coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of triclosan at a glassy carbon electrode modifies with carbon nanodots and chitosan
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Hong
Xu, Guifang
Gong, Lingshan
Yang, Caiping
Lin, Yanyu
Tong, Yuejin
Chen, Jinghua
Chen, Guonan
coredata.dc:description: Herein a conspicuously simple and sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nanocomposite architecture of the one-pot synthesized carbon nanodots (CNDs) and chitosan (CS) composite film was proposed for the determination of triclosan. The electrochemical response of triclosan was examined, demonstrated the irreversible electrochemical oxidation and one electron involved process of triclosan. Due to the good conductivity, large surface area and excellent electrochemical activity of CNDs, the CS and CNDs hybrid film extremely amplified the electrochemical response of triclosan, offering a highly sensitive electrochemical platform for triclosan detection. The detection limit of this electrochemical sensor for triclosan could be down to 9.2nM. The as-proposed sensor presented high sensitivity, low detection limit, wide dynamic response range, excellent stability and reproducibility. This simple and sensitive electrochemical sensor is promising in developing the disposable sensor for environmental monitoring and food inspection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triclosan
Chitosan
Carbon nanodots
Electrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CNDs through one step approach
Preparation of a CNDs-CS modified glassy carbon electrode
Characterization of electrochemical behavior of CNDs-CS/GCE
Effects of CNDs and CS upon the electrochemical response
Cyclic voltammetric behavior of triclosan at the CNDs-CS/GCE
Diffusion coefficient and standard heterogeneous rate constant of triclosan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal approach to CNDs and its TEM image, UV–vis adsorption (a) and PL spectra (b).

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes in 5mmoldm−3 Fe(CN)6 3− solution containing 0.1mmoldm−3 KCl (pH 7.0). Scan rate: 100mVs−1. (B) Nyquist plots of 3mmoldm−3 Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution containing 0.1moldm−3 KCl. (a) GCE, (b) CS/GCE, (c) CNDs-CS/GCE.
Cyclic voltammograms of 0.1mM triclosan at GCE (a), CS/GCE (b) and CNDs-CS/GCE (c) in 0.1moldm−3 PBS (pH 7). Cyclic voltammograms of CNDs-CS/GCE (d) in 0.1moldm−3 PBS (pH 7). Scan rate: 100mVs−1.
(A) Plot of Q–t curve of CNDs-CS/GCE in 0.1moldm−3 pH 7 PBS containing 0.1mmoldm−3 triclosan after background subtracted. (B) Plot of Q–t 1/2 curve on CNDs-CS/GCE.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of 0.1mmoldm−3 triclosan at CNDs-CS/GCE with different scan rates. Curves a–h is obtained at 5, 15, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125 and150mVs−1, respectively. (B) The plot for the dependence of peak current on the square root of scan rate. (C) The relationship between E pa and lnν.
(A) Cyclic voltammograms of 0.5mg/L triclosan at CNDs-CS/GCE under different pH 5, 6, 7, 7.5 and 8. (B) Effect of the pH on the oxidation current and oxidation potential. Scan rate: 100mVs−1.
Detection of triclosan in real samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of triclosan at a glassy carbon electrode modifies with carbon nanodots and chitosan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical engineering, Fujian Normal University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Education Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection for Food Safety, and Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Longyan Quality Inspection Institute of Product, Longyan, Fujian 364000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein a conspicuously simple and sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nanocomposite architecture of the one-pot synthesized carbon nanodots (CNDs) and chitosan (CS) composite film was proposed for the determination of triclosan. The electrochemical response of triclosan was examined, demonstrated the irreversible electrochemical oxidation and one electron involved process of triclosan. Due to the good conductivity, large surface area and excellent electrochemical activity of CNDs, the CS and CNDs hybrid film extremely amplified the electrochemical response of triclosan, offering a highly sensitive electrochemical platform for triclosan detection. The detection limit of this electrochemical sensor for triclosan could be down to 9.2nM. The as-proposed sensor presented high sensitivity, low detection limit, wide dynamic response range, excellent stability and reproducibility. This simple and sensitive electrochemical sensor is promising in developing the disposable sensor for environmental monitoring and food inspection.

ce:section.ce:para: Triclosan (2,2,4′-trichloro-2′-hydroxydiphenyl ether) is always utilized as an effective antibacterial and antifungal agent. So, triclosan is widely used in a variety of common household products, including soaps, mouthwashes, toothpastes, dish detergents, deodorants and hand sanitizers, and is infused in an increasing number of consumer products, such as bedding, socks and trash bags [1]. Therefore, the ingestion of trace amounts of triclosan in human being from various routes is inevitable. Recent reports demonstrated that tricolsan can combine with chlorine in tap water to form chloroform, which is classified by the United States Environmental Protection Agency as probable human carcinogen [2]. Furthermore, some researches also indicated that the formed chloroform can convert into dioxins, which is extremely toxic and is very potent endocrine disruptor [3,4]. A great deal of concern over the potential harm caused by tricolsan and its by-products towards environment, endocrine, allergy and health is given by the public. Hence, in August 2009, the Canadian Medical Association asked the Canadian government to ban triclosan use in household products under concerns of creating bacterial resistance and producing dangerous side products (chloroform). Triclosan safety is currently under review by the American Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [5].

Hence, it is crucial to develop rapid, sensitive and selective analytical methods for triclosan determination. Till now, some instrumental methods for tricolsan determination including gas chromatography-atomic emission [6], gas chromatography-ion trap mass spectrometry [7], liquid chromatography [8,9], chemiluminescence [10] and electrochemical method [11–17] have been developed. Since triclosan contains the construction of phenolic hydroxyl, it is strongly suggested that this compound could undergo oxidation process at the suitable sensing interface under proper conditions. Moreover, compared with other described methods, the direct electrochemical method for triclosan analysis, as a simple, rapid, cost-less, and sensitive alternative, is drawing increasing attention.

Cyclic voltammetry was firstly utilized to investigate the electrochemical behavior of triclosan by Pemberton and Hart at the screen printed electrode, in which the pK a and the oxidation reaction of triclosan were reaffirmed. Furthermore, triclosan monophosphate was found to be electrochemically inactive [11]. After this investigation, the researches related to electrochemical investigation for triclosan were then emerged gradually. Safavi et al. developed an electrochemical determination method for triclosan on mercury electrode [12]. A novel approach based on hydrotrope-driven disruption of the micellar encapsulants for the release of electroactive antimicrobials into the aqueous continuous phase was attempted by Phani and co-workers. This approach was examined by the direct voltammetric analysis of triclosan [13]. Wu and co-workers developed the multiwall carbon nanotube (MWCNT) film for rapid detection of triclosan, and even used the proposed sensor to detect tricolan in different toothpaste samples. Benefited from the potential electrocatalysis of MWCNT, the limit of detection of this electrochemical sensor could be down to 57nM [14]. It should be noted that Marken and co-workers have done considerable work to investigate triclosan. Amiri et al. fabricated the carbon particles and poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) formed film via the layer-by-layer process. By employing this composite film modified electrode, this sensor could be sensitive to multiply charged hydrophobic anions. Hence, it could obviously enhance the electrochemical response of triclosan, and be re-used by simple desorption into organic solvent [15]. Marken and co-workers developed an electrochemical platform functionalized with the chemically surface modified carbon nanoparticles for extraction an detection of triclosan and benzophenone-3(2-hydroxy-4-methoxy-benzophenone). Surface functionalization was found play a key role in extraction and detection efficiency [16]. Bonne et al. utilized thin film modified electrode formed by cellulose and poly-(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) for the accumulation and detection of triclosan [17]. However, to the best of our knowledge, electrochemical detection of triclosan utilizing the CNDs based hybrid film has not been published.

Herein, this paper described the development and application of CS and CNDs composite film modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for determination of trace amounts of triclosan, demonstrating that the hybrid film could not only significantly enhance the oxidation response of triclosan, but also depress its oxidation overpotential. Hence, based on this observation, a newly quantitative method for rapid, simple and sensitive determination of triclosan was developed, and the proposed method was employed to detect triclosan in toothpaste, mouthwash and hand sanitizer samples.

Triclosan was purchased from Aladdin Inc. (Shanghai, China). Chitosan (CS) and glucose, l-ascorbic acid was obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA) and used without further purification. Other reagents were of analytical regent grade. A 1.0% chitosan (w/v) solution was prepared by dissolving chitosan flakes in hot 0.05M acetic acid. The solution was cooled to room temperature and stirred at 2500rpm for 3h. The water used for the preparation of the solution was purified using a water purifier (China) purification system.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed with a CHI 760 electrochemical analyzer (Shanghai Chenghua Instrument Co., China). Voltammetric procedures were carried out with a three-electrode system comprising a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl reference electrode (sat. KCl) and a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, φ3mm), which was served as the working electrode. The pH measurements were carried out on PHS-3C exact digital pH metre (Shanghai Leici Co. Ltd., China), which was calibrated with standard pH buffer solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CNDs through one step approach
ce:section.ce:para:
CNDs were synthesized according to the previous report as it showed in Fig. 1
[18]. Briefly, 1.1g l-ascorbic acid was dissolved in 25mL deionized water and 25mL ethanol to form a homogeneous solution. Then, 25mL as-prepared solution was transferred into autoclave and heated at 180°C for 4h and then cooled to room temperature naturally. The dark brown solution was extracted with dichloromethane. The water phase solution was dialyzed by employing cellulose ester dialysis membranes for three days to remove all impurity molecules. At last, a yellow CNDs aqueous solution was obtained for the subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of a CNDs-CS modified glassy carbon electrode
ce:section.ce:para: A GCE was polished with 0.3, 0.05μm alumina particles on chamois leather in sequence, then washed successively with 1:20 (v/v) nitric acid, ethanol and water in an ultrasonic bath, and dried in air before use. In order to form CNDs-CS composite film, an appropriate amount of 1.0% chitosan solution was added into the CNDs solution, vigorous ultrasonication was then employed. Finally, the resulting mixture was then deposited onto GCE, and kept in an oven at 60°C for 25min.

Solution containing toothpaste, mouthwash and hand sanitizer samples were prepared as followed. Firstly, 0.5000g samples (toothpaste, mouthwash and hand sanitizer) were dissolved in 25.00mL methanol with vigorous sonication. Then the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 15,000 g for 15min. After filtrated, the liquid phase was collected for subsequent application. Before experiment, 2.5mL resultant solution was mixed with 2.5mL 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-obtained CNDs were shown in Fig. 1, revealing that the CNDs exhibited irregular shapes with a size distribution from 2.0 to 8.0nm with monodispersion. And typical UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CNDs were showed in Fig. 1. A strong absorption band appeared at 263nm with a narrow full width half maximum (fwhm) of 40nm. When the CNDs were excited at 363nm, the PL spectra showed a broad and symmetrical emission peak at 419nm. These values were consistent with previous reports [19–21]. Furthermore, it was found the PL spectra of CNDs are depended on the excitation wavelength, owning to the size heterogeneity and distribution of difference emissive sites on CNDs. Moreover, the CNDs aqueous solution under ambient light was showed in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of electrochemical behavior of CNDs-CS/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 described the cyclic voltammograms of 5mM Fe(CN)6 3− in 0.1M KCl obtained at GCE (a), CS/GCE (b), CNDs-CS/GCE (c). The redox peak current at CS/GCE benefited from the positive charge surface of CS film was higher than that at GCE. Due to the appearance of the positive charge surface of CS/GCE, the electrostatic attraction between Fe(CN)6 3− and the positively charged sensing platform facilitated Fe(CN)6 3− diffusion to the electrode surface. After modifying the GCE with CNDs and CS compound, the peak currents increased sequentially. Moreover, the corresponding peak current of this modified electrode was nearly five times greater than that of bare GCE. With the integration of nano-scale CNDs and CS, the synergistic effect then formed at this electrochemical platform, leading to the increase of the adsorption, electrochemically active sites and the effective electrode area. Further characterization for various modified electrode was carried out as it depicted in Fig. 2B. A well define semi-circle at higher frequencies were obtained at all these electrodes. When CS was modified on the surface of GCE, the resistance to charge transfer (R ct, 140Ω) was then substantially decreased than that case at GCE (610Ω), indicating the smaller interface electron transfer resistance. Moreover, after CNDs and CS formed composite film was introduced onto GCE, the interface electron transfer resistance (50Ω) was much smaller than other electrodes. The appearance of CNDs in the modified film could depress the resistance of the sensing platform in the solid and solution system, thus an accelerated rate of the electron transfer of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− was obtained. This was also strongly proved that CNDs and CS composite film could be a promising electrochemical platform for sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CNDs and CS upon the electrochemical response
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical response of the CNDs-CS film modified electrode to the Fe(CN)63−/4− redox system was expected to be affected by the amount of CNDs and CS on the sensing interface, which could be controlled by using the same volume of the modified solution with different volume fractions of CNDs and CS. It was found that along with the increased volume fraction of CNDs, the CNDs-CS sensing platform could not only amplify the current response, but result in the improvement of the electrochemical reversibility. However, when the volume fraction of CNDs was beyond 4.0%, the relative current intensity dramatically decreased. Also, the influence of CS on the electrochemical response was tested. Similar to the effect of CNDs on the electrochemical response, the increased concentration of CS could obviously improve the current intensity and the electrochemical reversibility of this sensor. And when the concentration of CS was beyond 0.4% (w/v), the electrochemical response decreased. Thus, in order to achieve the optimized electrochemical platform, we chose 4.0% of CNDs (v/v) and 0.4% of CS (w/v) as the modified layer components in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetric behavior of triclosan at the CNDs-CS/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical response of triclosan at GCE, CS/GCE and CNDs-CS/GCE were examined using cyclic voltammetry in triclosan solution. As shown in Fig. 3 , an obvious irreversible oxidation of triclosan was obtained at GCE, CS/GCE and CNDs-CS/GCE, which was in total agreement with previous reports [11,14,15]. This could be attributed to the oxidation of the phenolic hydroxyl contained construction of triclosan as followed:

It was clear that the appearance of CS increased the peak current of triclosan and displayed a substantial negative shift of the anodic peak potential, indicating that CS backbone exhibited obviously catalytic activity towards triclosan electro-oxidation due to the electrostatic action between the positively charged CS and the negatively charge triclosan. After CNDs were doped into CS film, the oxidation current of triclosan was further enhanced suggesting more facile electron transfer for triclosan oxidation process. Such promoted triclosan oxidation rate may be related to the oxygen rich groups and the well conduction of CNDs. Benefited from the unique properties of CNDs and CS, the CS backbone together with CNDs introduced obviously sensitized effect toward the electrochemical oxidation of triclosan, in particular, toward the increase of the reactive sites, conductivity, and surface area. Hence, this electrochemical platform based on CS and CNDs offered the enhanced determination sensitivity for triclosan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diffusion coefficient and standard heterogeneous rate constant of triclosan
ce:section.ce:para:
Chronocoulometry was employed to determine the diffusion coefficient of triclosan on the CNDs-CS/GCE. After subtracting the background in the PBS, the charge on CNDs-CS/GCE (Q) against the time (t) could be described as the following equation given by Anson [22]:
Q ( t ) =
2 n F A c
D
1 / 2
t
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
+
Q
ads
where A is the surface area of the working electrode, D is the diffusion coefficient, Q ads is Faradic charge. Other symbols have their normal meaning. By investigating the cyclic voltammograms of CNDs-CS/GCE in K3[Fe(CN)6] solution with varied scan rate, the slope of the linear regression between current and the square root of the scan rate could be described by Randles–Sevcik equation. Thus, the electrochemistry active area of CNDs-CS/GCE could be calculated to 0.261cm2. As it described in Fig. 4 , based on the slope of the linear relationship between Q and t 1/2, diffusion coefficient of triclosan could be calculated to be 1.3867×10−6 cm2 s−1. In addition, the standard heterogeneous rate constant (k s) for totally irreversible oxidation of triclosan at the CNDs-CS/GCE could be calculated based on following equation [23]:
k s
= 2.4 ×
exp
− 0.02 F
R T
⋅
D
1 / 2
⋅
(
E P
−
E
P / 2
)
− 1 / 2
⋅
υ
1 / 2
where E p and E p/2 represent the peak potential and the potential at which I = I p/2 in linear sweep voltammetry, respectively. In this report, E p − E p/2 =58.4mV, D =1.3867×10−6 cm2 s−1, υ =100mVs−1, and T =298K. Therefore, k s was calculated to be 1.71×10−3 cms−1, illustrating a relative fast electrode reaction process of triclosan on the electrochemical platform based on CNDs and CS.

The effect of scan rate on the oxidation of triclosan was also investigated. Fig. 5a revealed the cyclic voltammograms of 0.1mM triclosan at CNDs-CS/GCE with different scan rate. The anodic peak current increased gradually with the increase of scan rate. As shown in Fig. 5, the oxidation peak current of triclosan increased linearly with the square root of scan rate in the range of 5–150mVs−1, which indicated that the electro-oxidation of triclosan on CNDs-CS/GCE was a typical diffusion controlled process. Moreover, with the increased scan rate, the oxidation potential of triclosan shifted positively. The relationship between the oxidation potential and the natural logarithm of scan rate could be drawn in Fig. 5c. A well linear regression equation could be obtained in the scan rate range from 5 to 150mVs−1. In the irreversible electrochemical process, the relationship between the potential and scan rate could be described as following equation by Laviron [24]
E P
=
E 0
+
R T
α n F
ln
R T
k 0
α n F
+
R T
α n F
ln ν
where α is the electron transfer coefficient, n is the number of transfer electron, k 0 is the standard heterogeneous rate constant, R,T and F have their usual significance. Generally, in the totally irreversible electrochemical reaction, the value of αn can be easily calculated from the slope of E pa −ln ν. In this system, the slope was 0.04. Therefore, the value of αn is 0.64. According to foregoing research, n was confirmed as 1, hence, α can be calculated to be 0.64. Therefore, the calculated α was in the range from 0.4 to 0.8, which indicated the irreversible process of this electrochemical reaction [17,19].

Fig. 6 demonstrated the influence of the pH of buffer solution on the electro-oxidation peak potential and its corresponding anodic current of triclosan at CNDs-CS/GCE. With the pH value of buffer solution increased from 5.0 to 8.0, the anodic potential decreased gradually. When the pH value increased from 5.0 to 7.0, the anodic peak current of triclosan increased. Nevertheless, when the pH was beyond 7.0, the peak current conversely decreased. This phenomenon was probably due to the dissociation of the phenolic moiety to produce the corresponding anion. Therefore, considering the sensitive determination for triclosan pH 7.0 PBS was chose for the subsequent analytical experiments.

The determination of triclosan utilizing CNDs-CS/GCE was performed with LSV. Voltammetric peaks provided concentration information over the range from 10nM to 1.0mM and an estimated detection limit of 9.2nM for triclosan. The limit of detection in this report was lower than previous studies employing electrochemical method [12,14]. The stability of CNDs-CS/GCE was tested by measuring the response of this sensor in 0.03mM triclosan solution every 3 days. The results demonstrated that this sensor could keep 89.9% of its initial response after 12 days. Additionally, the relative standard deviation of five parallel measurements for 0.03mM triclosan employing this sensor was 4.8%. All these results suggested that the CNDs-CS/GCE had good reproducibility and stability.

Some inorganic ions, for example, 40-fold concentration of Na+, K+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, SO4 2−, Cl−, NO3 − and PO4 3− had no influence on the determination of triclosan. Furthermore, CNDs-CS/GCE was utilized to evaluate the detail properties in practical analytical applications. The results were shown in Table 1 . Triclosan was not found in mouthwash or hand sanitizer by applying CNDs-CS/GCE. Only 0.0030mg/g triclosan was found in toothpaste by this electrochemical method. The recovery was in the range from 103.7% to 117.4%, demonstrating that this sensor might be sufficient for practical applications.

Herein, one sensitive and reliable electrochemical platform was applied to study the electrochemical oxidation of triclosan utilizing CNDs and chitosan as electrode modification hybrid film. Owning to the unique properties of CNDs and CS, CNDs-CS/GCE possessed large surface area and highly efficient catalysis towards the electro-oxidation of triclosan, which also resulted in promoting electron kinetics. Hence, based on the greatly enhanced electrochemical reactivity of triclosan, quantitative detection of triclosan was performed at the proposed electrode. The dynamic range covered 5 orders of magnitudes. This CNDs-CS/GCE sensing interface integrated excellent merits, such as easy in fabrication, high stability and sensitivity etc. Due to its flexibility, it could hold huge promise for further electrochemical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2011J05020), Education Department of Fujian Province (JA11062), Fujian Normal University Outstanding Young Teacher Research Fund Projects (fjsdjk2012068) and National Nature Sciences Foundation of China (41076059).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Triclosan at Household Products Database, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.
U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Triclosan: What Consumers Should Know, 2010. (http://www.fda.gov/ForConsumers/ConsumerUpdates/ucm205999.htm).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry
